coredata.dc:title: Bifunctional nanoprobe for dual-mode detection based on blue emissive iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots as a peroxidase-mimic platform
coredata.dc:creator: Alqahtani, Yahya S.
Mahmoud, Ashraf M.
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
coredata.dc:description: In this study, iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Fe/N@C-dots) were fabricated via one pot hydrothermal method. The as-synthesized Fe/N@C-dots exhibited unique properties such as high catalytic activity and fluorescence quantum yield (33.8%). The peroxidase-like activity of Fe/N@C-dots was used to detect H2O2 in the presence of phenol and 4-aminoantipyrine (4-AMAP) to yield a pink colored product (quinoneimine chromogen), which was measured at λ max = 505 nm (colorimetric method). Moreover, the pink colored product quenched the fluorescence of Fe/N@C-dots via inner-filter effect and static quenching (fluorometric method). The enzyme like activity of Fe/N@C-dots was cascaded with the enzymatic activity of glucose oxidase (GOx) to evaluate the practical application of Fe/N@C-dots to develop dual-channel sensor (fluorometric and colorimetric) for high selective and sensitive detection of glucose. It was found that the absorption intensities were increased linearly with glucose concentration in the range of 2.13–130 μM with LOD (S/N = 3) of 0.58 μM, while fluorescence intensities were decreased linearly after addition of glucose in the range of 1.33–140 μM with LOD (S/N = 3) of 0.36 μM. The Fe/N@C-dots/4-AMAP/phenol/GOx system along with the fluorometric method were applied efficiently to determine glucose in many samples including human serum, urine, and saliva with acceptable recoveries % in the range of 93.8–106.1% and RSD % not more than 3.7%, suggesting the reliability of the as-fabricated biosensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Glucose
Peroxidase-like activity
Phenol
4-Aminoantipyrine
Colorimetric
Fluorometric

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Fe/N@C-dots)
Preparation of human serum, urine, and saliva samples
Peroxidase-like activity of Fe/N@C-dots and mechanism of quenching
Dual assay of H2O2 and glucose

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful to the Deanship of Scientific Research at Najran University for funding this work under the National Research Priorities funding program grant code ( NU/NRP/MRC/11/5 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of Fe/N@C-dots (Inset is HRTEM), (B) size distribution of Fe/N@C-dots, (C) FT-IR spectra of (a) N@C-dots and (b) Fe/N@C-dots, and (D) Raman spectra of (a) N@C-dots and (b) Fe/N@C-dots.
PXRD patterns of (a) N@C-dots and (b) Fe/N@C-dots.

The catalytic activity of Fe/N@C-dots at different conditions including pH (a), incubation temperature (b), amount of catalyst (c), incubation time (d), concentration of phenol (e), concentration of 4-AMAP (f), and volume of NaAC-HAC buffer (pH 3.6). Each parameter was measured at optimum values of other parameters.

(a) Colorimetric responses of Fe/N@C-dots/4-AMAP/phenol system after addition of different amounts of H2O2 (5.0–130.0 μM), inset of (a) is colors of chromogen after addition of different amounts of H2O2. (B) Calibration plot between absorbance and concentration of H2O2. (c) Emission responses of Fe/N@C-dots after treating with different amounts of H2O2 (5.0–140.0 μM) in the presence of 4-AMAP/phenol system, inset of (c) is fluorescence photos under 365 nm UV-lamp. (d) Calibration plot between F°-F and concentration of H2O2. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Colorimetric responses of Fe/N@C-dots/4-AMAP/phenol/GOx system after addition of different amounts of glucose (5.0–130.0 μM). (b) Calibration plot between absorbance and concentration of glucose. (c) Emission responses of Fe/N@C-dots after treating with different amounts of glucose (5.0–140.0 μM) in the presence of 4-AMAP/phenol/GOx system. (d) Calibration plot between F°-F and concentration of glucose.

Steady-state kinetic analysis using the Michaelis-Menten model (A and B) and the Lineweaver-Burk model (C and D) for Fe/N@C-dots. The concentration of TMB was 0.13 mM and the H2O2 concentration was varied (0.05–1.78 mM) (A and C). The concentration of H2O2 was 0.9 mM and the TMB concentration was varied (0.02–0.58 mM) (B and D).

Selectivity of colorimetric (A) and fluorometric methods (B) for determination of glucose in the presence of potential interfering species. The concentration of glucose and interfering species are 75 μM and 0.5 mM, respectively.
The main steps for colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode detection of H2O2 and glucose.
Comparison of the analytical parameters of the proposed colorimetric and fluorometric glucose sensor to those of previously reported glucose sensors.
Comparison of Km and Vmax between Fe/N@C-dots and other catalysts.
Tolerance ratios of interferences for the detection of glucose.

row.entry: Ratio in physiological body fluids (Glu 7.8 mM) *

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *All concentrations were collected at the highest end of the normal range in blood.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of glucose in serum, urine, and saliva samples using fluorometric method (n = 4).

head.ce:title: Bifunctional nanoprobe for dual-mode detection based on blue emissive iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots as a peroxidase-mimic platform

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, College of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, College of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Fe/N@C-dots) were fabricated via one pot hydrothermal method. The as-synthesized Fe/N@C-dots exhibited unique properties such as high catalytic activity and fluorescence quantum yield (33.8%). The peroxidase-like activity of Fe/N@C-dots was used to detect H2O2 in the presence of phenol and 4-aminoantipyrine (4-AMAP) to yield a pink colored product (quinoneimine chromogen), which was measured at λ max = 505 nm (colorimetric method). Moreover, the pink colored product quenched the fluorescence of Fe/N@C-dots via inner-filter effect and static quenching (fluorometric method). The enzyme like activity of Fe/N@C-dots was cascaded with the enzymatic activity of glucose oxidase (GOx) to evaluate the practical application of Fe/N@C-dots to develop dual-channel sensor (fluorometric and colorimetric) for high selective and sensitive detection of glucose. It was found that the absorption intensities were increased linearly with glucose concentration in the range of 2.13–130 μM with LOD (S/N = 3) of 0.58 μM, while fluorescence intensities were decreased linearly after addition of glucose in the range of 1.33–140 μM with LOD (S/N = 3) of 0.36 μM. The Fe/N@C-dots/4-AMAP/phenol/GOx system along with the fluorometric method were applied efficiently to determine glucose in many samples including human serum, urine, and saliva with acceptable recoveries % in the range of 93.8–106.1% and RSD % not more than 3.7%, suggesting the reliability of the as-fabricated biosensor.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is a vital biomolecule in cellular signal transduction [1]. However, excessive production of this small molecule is related to serious cell damage, resulting in some diseases such as diabetes, Alzheimer's disease, inflammation, and cancer [2,3]. Glucose is an essential energy source for living organisms and is considered a metabolic intermediate for forming other biomolecules. Abnormal levels of glucose in human urine, blood, tears, and other body fluids result in the development of metabolism related diseases such as cardiovascular disorders, obesity, and diabetes mellitus [4–6]. Based on these findings, it is essential to determine these compounds with sufficient sensitivity and selectivity in different biological fluids. Many methods have been reported for the determination of H2O2 and glucose such as colorimetry [7,8], fluorometry [9,10], and chromatography [11]. Chromatographic technique is tedious, consumes toxic organic solvents, and needs highly expert technicians [12–14]. Although, the colorimetric and fluorometric methods have many advantages such as low cost, simplicity, sensitivity, and good selectivity. However, the above mentioned methods depend on single-mode detection that may be influenced by external factors and environmental interferences, affecting the accuracy of the method. As a result, colorimetric/fluorometric dual-mode detection provides a more reliable strategy for accurate determination of H2O2 and glucose.

Natural enzymes are substances extensively used in bioassays as a result of their high efficiency and selectivity in catalyzing chemical reactions. However, the susceptibility of natural enzymes to environmental variations, intolerance to organic solvents, high cost, and difficult to recover limits their applications [15]. In contrast, artificial enzymes have many advantages including environmental stability, high catalytic activity, low cost, and ease of preparation [16,17]. Various nanomaterials have been proven to possess enzyme-like activity including carbon variants, noble metals, metal oxides, and others [18,19]. Among them, carbon dots (C-dots), a zero-dimensional nanomaterial, have emerged multiple applications due to ultrasmall size (below 10 nm), ease of dispersion, water solubility, outstanding catalytic properties, and good optical properties [20–25]. Small size of C-dots will give it more exposed active sites and ease of functionalization. It can be produced in a high yield and in a simple way; besides; it is eco-friendly [26,27]. Poor electron transfer exhibited by pristine C-dots decreases their catalytic activity and limits their applications. As a result, heteroatom doping e.g. nitrogen is widely applied to enhance their internal electronic environment [28,29]. Moreover, transition metal doping increases the active sites through peroxidase-like activity. Iron-doped C-dots show higher peroxidase-catalytic activity than other transition metals doped C-dots [30,31]. As a conclusion, doping of both nitrogen (an example of heteroatom) and Fe (an example of transition metal) increases the peroxidase-like activity and the fluorescence quantum yield of C-dots. Recently, there have been some reports about Fe and N co-doped carbon dots (Fe/N@C-dots) in the detection of biomolecules. Weng et al. fabricated Fe/N@C-dots as a peroxidase-mimic for picomolar detection of cysteine [32]. Liang et al. synthesized Fe/N@C-dots from Passiflora edulis as a peroxidase-mimetic for colorimetric determination of uric acid [33].

Herein, a dual-channel sensor was fabricated for colorimetric and fluorometric determination of H2O2 and glucose. The sensor consists of iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Fe/N@C-dots) as a peroxidase-mimic for sensitive and selective detection of H2O2 and glucose. In the presence of Fe/N@C-dots as a peroxidase-like, the oxidation coupling of 4-aminoantipyrine (4-AMAP) and phenol in the presence of H2O2 to form pink to red colored product (λ max = 505 nm) was enhanced. The resultant colored product quenched the fluorescence emission of Fe/N@C-dots via inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching. The enzyme like activity of Fe/N@C-dots was cascaded with the enzymatic activity of glucose oxidase (GOx) to evaluate the practical application of Fe/N@C-dots to develop a dual-channel sensor for high selective and sensitive detection of glucose. Compared to other reported sensors, the proposed dual-mode sensor has many advantages including wide dynamic linear range, high selectivity, easy to process, and reliability. Moreover, the proposed detection method is preferable and desirable since it can determine the analyte signals at two different wavelengths using two different techniques unlike the traditional methods that measure either the fluorescence or colorimetric intensity only at a single wavelength.

Glucose oxidase (GOx, G7141-10KU), cysteine (≥97%), dopamine (≥97%), uric acid (≥97%), glycine (≥97%), 4-aminoantipyrine (4-AMAP, ≥ 98%), phenol (≥98%), lysine (≥98%) and ascorbic acid (≥98%) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, Steinheim, Germany. Glucose (≥98%), Sucrose (≥97%), maltose (≥97%), lactose (≥98%), CH3COOH (HAc, AR), CH3COONa (NaAc, AR), HCl (AR), NaOH (AR), ferric chloride (FeCl3·6H2O, AR), and Na2EDTA (AR) were purchased from El-Nasr for Chemicals Intermediates, Cairo, Egypt. Double distilled water (DDW) was used along the whole study.

Shimadzu 1601 PC UV–Vis (Tokyo, Japan) and FluoroMate spectrofluorometer (Scinco, Korea) were used to record absorption and emission spectra, respectively. Morphology of Fe/N@C-dots was studied using Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan). Nicolet™ iS™10 spectrometer (Thermo Electron Scientific Instruments Corp., Madison, WI, USA) was used to reveal the main functional groups of Fe/N@C-dots. Philips X-ray diffractometer PW 1710 was used to analyze the peak diffractions in the Fe/N@C-dots (Philips-FEI, Netherlands). EDX was evaluated using NEX QC + QuantEZ (Oxford, USA). Raman spectra were recorded using Micro-Raman spectrometer (UK). X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, ESCA Ulvac-PHI 1600, PHI Quantum 2000 XPS system, Physical Electronics, USA) was used to reveal the surface functional groups of Fe/N@C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Fe/N@C-dots)
ce:section.ce:para: The Fe/N@C-dots were fabricated through one-step hydrothermal method. Briefly, 1.5 g FeCl3·6H2O and 2 g of Na2EDTA were dissolved in 250 mL DDW. After stirring for 10 min, the mixture was autoclaved at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooling at room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 20 min. After that, the solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3000 Da) for 2 days while changing the DDW every 12 h. The as-synthesized Fe/N@C-dots were freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C. Nitrogen carbon dots (N@C-dots) were prepared using the same steps without addition of FeCl3·6H2O. Moreover, pristine carbon dots (C-dots) were prepared using the same steps while citric acid was used as a carbon source.

200 μL H2O2 (different concentrations) was mixed with 150 μL 10.0 mM phenol, 100 μL 10.0 mM 4-AMAP, 300 μL NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 3.6), and 250 μL 2.0 mg mL−1 Fe/N@C-dots. After mixing, the solution was completed to 3.0 mL with DDW and incubated for 20 min at 40 °C. After that, the absorbance of the resultant solution was measured at λ max = 505 nm, while fluorescence spectra were recorded at λ em. = 440 nm after excitation at 365 nm.

(i) To generate H
2
O
2 : 200 μL glucose (different concentrations) was mixed with 10 μL of GOx (5.0 mg mL−1) and 50 μL NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 5.5). After that, the solution was incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. (ii) To perform determination: 150 μL 10.0 mM phenol, 100 μL 10.0 mM 4-AMAP, 300 μL NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 3.6), and 250 μL of 2.0 mg mL−1 Fe/N@C-dots were mixed with the glucose and GOx mixture in the first step. After mixing, the solution was completed to 3.0 mL with DDW and incubated for 20 min at 40 °C. The absorbance of the resultant solution was measured at λ max = 505 nm, while fluorescence spectra were recorded at λ em. = 440 nm after excitation at 365 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of human serum, urine, and saliva samples
ce:section.ce:para: Blood plasma samples were obtained from Assiut University Hospital by collecting from healthy and non-smoking volunteers with informed consents. For preparation of human serum samples, 0.5 mL blood plasma was mixed with 0.75 mL acetonitrile and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 20 min. Finally, the supernatant was collected and stored at 4 °C [34,35]. For urine sample, 1.0 mL was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 20 min. Then, the urine sample was filtered using medium filter paper [36,37]. For saliva sample, 0.5 mL of saliva was collected in disposable tubes. Then, it was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 15 min, and then filtered [38]. The experimental protocol was conducted according to the Egyptian regulations and approved by the Institutional Human Ethics Committee, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt. Informed consents were obtained from all participants for this study.

Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) was used to characterize Fe/N@C-dots morphologically where they exhibit spherical shape and narrow size distribution with an average size of 3.7 nm (Fig. 1 A&B). The inset of Fig. 1A shows the HRTEM of Fe/N@C-dots with lattice fringes of 0.22 nm, which assigns to the [100] facet of sp2-graphitic carbon [39]. FT-IR of N@C-dots and Fe/N@C-dots were presented in Fig. 1C where they exhibit bands at 3470, 2800–2930, 1720, and 1630 cm−1, which assign to υ (OH, NH), υ (CH2), υ (CO), and δ (OH, NH), respectively [40–42]. Moreover, appearance of a new band at 520 cm−1 corresponds to the υ (Fe–O), suggesting the successful doping of Fe during the fabrication of Fe/N@C-dots. The Raman spectra shown in Fig. 1D exhibit two bands at 1390 and 1595 cm−1, which assign to the D-and G-bands, respectively. The ratios of D- and G-bands intensities of N@C-dots and Fe/N@C-dots were found to be 0.87 and 0.94, respectively, proving that the Fe/N@C-dots possessed more disordered carbon structures.

The PXRD patterns of Fe/N@C-dots exhibited a higher angle peak at ≈25.7° that corresponds to the graphitic carbon, confirming that the Fe was doped well to the carbon framework. Another weak peak was observed at ≈36.5°, which is attributed to the Fe–N phase, Fig. 2 [43]. These results confirmed the higher disordered graphite-like structure of Fe/N@C-dots. Fig. 3 shows the elemental analysis of Fe/N@C-dots with identifiable peaks of C, O, N, and Fe, proving successful doping of Fe.

X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was used to investigate the surface state and composition of Fe/N@C-dots (Fig. S1). Fig.S1a exhibits the existence of four main peaks of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and Fe 2p. Fig.S1b shows the high-resolution C1s spectra with three characteristic peaks of 284.6 eV, 285.8 eV, and 286.9 eV, which are assigned to CC, C–C, and CO/CN, respectively, proving the presence of conjugated structures. In high-resolution O1s spectra, there are four resolved peaks at 529.4 eV, 530.7 eV, 531.8 eV, and 532.3 eV are assigned to C–O, Fe–O, CO, and C–OH/C–O–C, respectively (Fig. S1c). The high-resolution N1s spectra show well resolved peaks at 397.3 eV, 399.2 eV, 403.7 eV, and 407.2 eV, which are attributed to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, graphitic N, and N-oxide, respectively. Pyrrolic and pyridinic N can form metal-nitrogen coordination bonds and may become active sites for the catalytic reaction (Fig. S1d). Moreover, the high-resolution Fe2p were located at 711.8 eV, 714.7 eV, 721.8.5 eV, and 723.7 eV, which are assigned to Fe2+ 2p3/2, Fe3+ satellite, Fe2+ 2p1/2, and Fe3+ 2p1/2, respectively (Fig. S1e). The results of XPS analysis are fairly consistent with a previous report [44].

The absorption and fluorescence spectra of Fe/N@C-dots are shown in Fig.S2A. It is seen that the as-synthesized Fe/N@C-dots show two prominent absorption bands at 230 and 310 nm, which are assigned to the π- π* and n–π* transitions, respectively. Moreover, the fluorescence emission of Fe/N@C-dots was recorded at λ em. of 440 nm after excitation at 365 nm. In addition, the emission intensities were varied with excitation wavelengths in the range of 330–400 nm, which might be due to the different size or surface functional groups of the as-fabricated Fe/N@C-dots [45,46]. Fig. S2B shows that the maximum emission was attained at 440 nm after excitation at 365 nm. The as-synthesized Fe/N@C-dots show blue emission under UV light compared to yellow color under daylight (inset of Fig.S2A).
Details for the calculation of QY with the corresponding figure (Fig. S3) were listed in Electronic Supplementary Materials (ESM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase-like activity of Fe/N@C-dots and mechanism of quenching
ce:section.ce:para: Phenol and 4-AMAP are oxidatively coupled in the presence of H2O2 as an oxidant to form a pink to red colored product (quinoneimine) with λ max = 505 nm. Fig.S4a exhibits UV–vis spectra of the interaction between phenol and 4-AMAP in the presence of H2O2 and acetate buffer (pH 3.6). An absorption band was observed in the system consisting of Fe/N@C-dots/H2O2/4-AMAP/phenol. However, in the absence of Fe/N@C-dots, no UV–vis absorption band was seen (Fig.S4b), suggesting the importance of the nanozyme for oxidative coupling between phenol, 4-AMAP, and H2O2. In order to prove the roles of 4-AMAP, phenol, H2O2, and Fe/N@C-dots, comparative studies including Fe/N@C-dots + H2O2+4-AMAP, Fe/N@C-dots+ 4-AMAP + phenol, and Fe/N@C-dots + H2O2+phenol combined systems were tested. Fig. S4c proves that only Fe/N@C-dots + H2O2+4-AMAP + phenol produced a well seen absorption band at λ max = 505 nm. Moreover, no absorption band was observed when N@C-dots and C-dots replaced Fe/N@C-dots, proving that pristine C-dots and N@C-dots without active Fe barely have peroxidase activity (Fig. S4d). It is important to mention that the role of nitrogen doping during the fabrication of Fe/N@C-dots is the enhancement of the fluorescence emission, which increases the sensitivity of the fluorometric detection. Fig. S4e suggested that the Fe and N doping may change the structure and surface state of the carbon core, leading to the enhancement of fluorescence emission intensity.

The quenching of Fe/N@C-dots emission by the phenol + H2O2+4-AMAP system is shown in Fig. S5a while other systems have no effect on the emission of Fe/N@C-dots. Fig. S5b shows an overlap between the absorption of the pink/red chromogen and the emission spectrum of Fe/N@C-dots, suggesting the quenching is attributed to the inner-filter effect (IFE) [47]. Fig.S5c exhibits the average lifetimes of Fe/N@C-dots before and after addition of chromogen. The average lifetimes were 8.32 and 8.39 ns for Fe/N@C-dots and Fe/N@C-dots/chromogen, respectively suggesting static quenching [48].

Stern Volmer equation was used to prove that the quenching of Fe/N@C-dots by chromogen was attributed to the static quenching. The formula of Stern Volmer can be summarized as follows: (F°/F)-1 = KQ= Kq τ0 [Q] where F° and F indicate the emission intensity in the absence and presence of chromogen (quencher), Q determines the concentration of chromogen, Kq is the quenching rate constant and τ0 is the fluorescence lifetime of the Fe/N@C-dots. The value of Kq for Fe/N@C-dots was found to be 7.69 × 1012 M−1 L s−1 which is much larger than the dynamic quenching constant (1.0 × 1010 M−1 L s−1), confirming that the quenching is related to the static quenching (Fig.S5d), [49]. In conclusion, the quenching mechanism is speculated to be inner-filter effect and static quenching.

To optimize the sensitivity of detection, several factors affecting the peroxidase-like activity of Fe/N@C-dots were optimized including pH, incubation temperature, amount of Fe/N–C-dots, and incubation time. From Fig. 4 , it was found that the optimum values are 3.6, 40 °C, 2.0 mg mL−1, and 20 min for pH, incubation temperature, amount of Fe/N–C-dots, and incubation time, respectively. It can be concluded that the peroxidase-like activity of Fe/N–C-dots was pH-dependent. Under acidic environment, the catalytic performance of Fe/N–C-dots was increased where the acidic conditions facilitate the Fenton-like chemical reaction facilitated by Fe doped in the skeleton of N–C-dots [50,51]. Moreover, increasing the pH value above 3.6 resulted in a decrease of the absorbance value, which might be due to decreasing the stability of the produced chromogen [29] (Fig. 4a). At higher temperatures, the responses were decreased that may be attributed to decomposition of H2O2 and the colored product (Fig. 4b). In addition, the effect of different concentrations of 250 μL Fe/N@C-dots was investigated; it was observed that the absorbance of the produced chromogen was increased by increasing the amount of Fe/N@C-dots until reaching the optimum value at 2.0 mg mL−1. Below this value, the amount of Fe/N@C-dots was not sufficient to favor a complete coupling between 4-AMAP and phenol. Above 2.0 mg mL−1, the absorbance values became constant, confirming that the optimum Fe/N@C-dots amount is 2.0 mg mL−1 (Fig. 4c). In addition, the effect of the incubation time on the formation of chromogen was studied (Fig. 4d). It was obvious that the optimum incubation time is 20 min; below this value, the absorption intensity is low as a result of incomplete reaction between the donor and the acceptor [29]. Moreover, concentrations of phenol and 4-AMPA were studied where it was found that absorbance values were increased with different concentrations of phenol (150 μL) and 4-AMAP (100 μL) until maximum value at 10.0 mM; then, leveled off (Fig. 4e&f). In addition, the volume of NaAC/HAC buffer (pH = 3.6) was also optimized where it was found that the optimum volume is 300 μL (Fig. 4g).

In order to enhance the activity of GOx for catalytic decomposition of glucose, some variables were studied including concentration of GOx, pH value, volume of NaAC-HAC buffer, and incubation time. From Fig.S6 it can be concluded that the optimum conditions are 5.0 mg ml-1, 5.5, 50 μL, and 30 min for GOx concentration, pH value, volume of NaAC-HAC buffer, and incubation time, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual assay of H2O2 and glucose
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 shows the main steps for the determination of H2O2 and glucose using a dual-mode based sensor. The Fe/N@C-dots as a peroxidase-mimetic can catalyze the oxidation of 4-AMAP and phenol with H2O2 to form red/pink colored product with λ max = 505 nm (basis of colorimetric method). In addition, the fluorescence of Fe/N@C-dots can be quenched by the red/pink colored product through inner-filter effect and static quenching (basis of fluorometric method). For glucose, it is essential to add glucose oxidase (GOx) to catalyze its decomposition to H2O2.

Fig. 5 shows the absorption and fluorescence spectra of the Fe/N@C-dots/4-AMAP/phenol system after the addition of different concentrations of H2O2 along with their corresponding calibration plots. It is clearly seen that the absorbance readings are increased with addition of H2O2 in the concentration range of 5.0–130.0 μM with LOQ (S/N = 10) and LOD (S/N = 3) of 2.17 and 0.65 μM, respectively (Fig. 5 a&b). For the fluorometric method, addition of different concentrations of H2O2 decreased the emission of Fe/N@ C-dots in the range of 5.0–140.0 μM with LOQ and LOD of 1.42 and 0.42 μM, respectively (Fig. 5 c&d). The linear regression equations for colorimetric and fluorometric methods are A = 0.122 + 0.0046CH2O2 (R2 = 0.9991) and (F°-F) = 1.02 + 0.0035CH2O2 (R2 = 0.9986) (F°: emission of Fe/N@ C-dots before H2O2 addition; F: emission of Fe/N@ C-dots after addition of H2O2), respectively. It is noteworthy mentioning that the fluorometric method is advantageous over the colorimetric method for H2O2 detection in terms of wide-linear range and low LOD value.

Fig. 6 shows the absorption and fluorescence spectra of the Fe/N@C-dots/4-AMAP/phenol/GOx system after addition of different glucose concentrations and their corresponding calibration plots. It is clearly seen that the absorbance readings are increased with the addition of glucose in the concentration range of 5.0–130.0 μM with LOQ (S/N = 10) and LOD (S/N = 3) of 2.13 and 0.58 μM, respectively (Fig. 6 a&b). For the fluorometric method, addition of glucose in the range of 5.0–140.0 μM decreased the emission intensity of Fe/N@C-dots with LOQ and LOD of 1.33 and 0.36 μM, respectively (Fig. 6 c&d). The linear regression equations for colorimetric and fluorometric methods are A = 0.099 + 0.0041Cglucose (R2 = 0.9985) and RFI = 1.00 + 0.0033Cglucose (R2 = 0.9976), respectively. It is noteworthy mentioning that the fluorometric method is advantageous over the colorimetric method for glucose detection in terms of wide-linear range and low LOD value.

To raise the importance of the as-prepared system, the analytical performance criteria of the system were compared with previously reported methods for the determination of glucose (Table 1 ). Results indicated that the as-fabricated sensor has satisfactory low LODs values and wide-linear ranges.

Six different batches of Fe/N@C-dots were prepared at different times under the same experimental conditions. The relative standard deviation
%
(
s t a n d a r d
d e v i a t i o n
o f
s i x
r e a d i n g s
A v e r a g e
o f
r e a d i n g s
× 100
) was calculated and found to be 3.3%, confirming the reproducibility of the Fe/N@C-dots as a peroxidase-mimetic. Moreover, the relative activity % (RA %) of Fe/N@C-dots was measured for 60 days where the platform restored its activity for this period with no significant decrease of RA %, proving the stability of Fe/N@C-dots over a long period. After this period (60 days), it was found that the RA % value was significantly decreased, which might be due to the change in the particle size of Fe/N@C-dots, affecting their catalytic activity.

The selectivity of the system was tested with the most likely interfering compounds, including those in bodily fluids. In order to assess the specificity of the colorimetric glucose sensing, specific interfering agents, such as sugars, amino acids, and metal ions (all at a final concentration of 0.5 mM) were added to the same glucose detection system. As shown in Fig. 8 , the results indicate a good selectivity of the colorimetric sensing system for glucose compared to other sugars (maltose, lactose, galactose, sucrose, and fructose). However, some substances such as cysteine, ascorbic acid, creatinine, dopamine, and epinephrine interfere with the detection process. The interference was observed for cysteine, dopamine, epinephrine, and ascorbic acid due to their ability to reduce OH radicals [65,66]. The insoluble creatinine might interfere within the reaction by blocking the active sites of Fe/N@C-dots. Therefore, we surveyed the concentration of the interference at that point, and the different intensity was just around 10% (Table 3 ). As seen in this Table, cysteine can interfere within the analysis during biofluid analysis under normal physiological conditions. As the concentration of glucose is high in a biological fluid, so the dilution of the sample may eliminate the interference from cysteine. Significantly, tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) can be used as a chromogenic reagent instead of 4-AMAP/phenol where it can be oxidized by H2O2 in the presence of Fe/N@C-dots to the oxidized TMB (ox-TMB) with a blue color at λ max = 652 nm. However, the overlapping between the quinoneimine chromogen (λ max = 505 nm) absorption spectrum and emission spectrum of Fe/N@C-dots (λ em. = 440 nm) is higher than ox-TMB (λ max = 652 nm) and Fe/N@C-dots. This means that the quenching of Fe/N@C-dots by quinoneimine chromogen is much higher than ox-TMB. Moreover, the extinction molar absorptivity of quinoneimine chromogen is higher than ox-TMB, increasing the sensitivity of the colorimetric method (Fig. S7) [29].

Fluorometry-based sensor was used to measure the amount of glucose in real samples due to higher sensitivity compared to colorimetric method. . As a consequence of the high amount of glucose in some biofluids (in mM level), the biological sample was diluted 1000 folds with NaAc-HAc buffer. The samples were spiked with different concentrations of glucose and the results were summarized in Table 4 . Recoveries of glucose from samples were in the range of 93.8–106.1% with RSD % less than 3.7%, referring to the accuracy of the proposed biosensor. Glucose level in serum was measured in hospital method using a glucometer where a drop of diluted serum was placed on a disposable test-strip. For urine and saliva samples, the glucose levels were measured using a conventional technique called Auto-analyzer, which is based on enzymatic reaction.

The research study presents a new approach for constructing a peroxidase-mimetic platform, which paves the way for point-of-care detection of glucose in biomedical diagnosis. In summary, iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Fe/N@C-dots) were synthesized via facile and one-pot hydrothermal method. It exhibits a decent peroxidase-like activity under appropriate reaction conditions. The Fe/N@C-dots were used in the presence of phenol/4-AMAP and phenol/4-AMAP/GOx for the colorimetric and fluorometric detections of H2O2 and glucose, respectively. The proposed system was efficiently applied for the fluorometric detection of glucose in serum, urine, and saliva samples with satisfactory results. The suggested system can be leveraged in analytical chemistry and point-of-care applications.

Dr. Mohamed M. El-Wekil: Conceptualization; Data curation; Formal analysis; Investigation; Methodology; Software; Supervision; Validation; Visualization; Writing - original draft; Writing - review & editing. Prof. Dr. Ashraf M. Mahmoud: Investigation; Software; Validation; Visualization; review & editing. Dr. Yahya S. Alqahtani: Resources; review & editing, funding.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Deanship of Scientific Research at Najran University for funding this work under the National Research Priorities funding program grant code (NU/NRP/MRC/11/5).
